eras.i: The rules of the game, written before any of us showed up. Empire by other names; the monetary fight Bryan lost twice to McKinley; a central bank whose accounting nobody has been able to argue with since; the 1929 crash that demonstrated the architecture in the hardest possible terms; a New Deal that, briefly, held the bust in place; and a postwar order built around a dollar pegged to gold.
eras.ii: After the war, an intelligence apparatus designed for a global contest. Where elected governments became inconvenient, it removed them. Iran. Guatemala. Indonesia. Chile. The body count from this period, gathered alongside the coups themselves, runs into the millions. By 1975 the public read enough of its own files to ask what else.
eras.iii: The dollar comes off gold. Reagan rewires the social compact at home, while Central America burns. September 2001 reopens the playbook for foreign wars, and the bailouts that follow run the opposite direction of the New Deal's.
eras.iv: Citizens United legalizes the money. Snowden documents the mass collection. The Epstein files surface and disappear. Gaza is funded, armed, and shielded. Palantir, OpenAI, and the data-center race come for the rest.
eras.v: From institutions to individuals. The earlier eras document systems; this one documents the people running them — named, on the record, with primary sources and witnesses. The question shifts from “who built this” to “is the person in the room reliable.”

anchors.y1898: The Spanish-American War. By 1898 the United States had taken Cuba, Puerto Rico, Guam, the Philippines, and Hawaii — a continental republic acquired an overseas empire in a single year, and built the institutions to run it. The framing of American power as primarily defensive starts to drift from the record at exactly this point.
anchors.y1900-mckinley: William McKinley defeats William Jennings Bryan for the second time. Bryan's “Cross of Gold” platform — bimetallism, free silver, looser money for the indebted agricultural classes — loses. McKinley's program — gold standard, high tariffs, overseas expansion — wins, and becomes the architecture the United States is built on. Frank Baum publishes The Wizard of Oz the same year; scholars since Henry Littlefield's 1964 essay have read it as a monetary allegory of that election (yellow brick road = gold, silver slippers = silver, the wizard = banks behind the curtain). Donald Trump has cited McKinley repeatedly since 2024 as his own model — the tariffs, the territorial ambitions, the same argument wearing different clothes.
anchors.y1913: The Federal Reserve Act creates a central banking system run by a board of governors with regional reserve banks owned by member commercial banks. The architecture mixes public and private authority over the money supply in a way that has been argued about ever since. The mechanic that has nagged at critics from Bryan to Ellen Brown — that commercial banks create the money when they make loans, but do not create the interest — is filed below alongside the Bank of England's 2014 paper documenting it in plain language.
anchors.y1929-crash: From its September 3, 1929 peak of 381 to its July 8, 1932 trough of 41.22, the Dow falls 89%. The 1920s run-up was substantially leveraged: roughly 300 million shares were carried on margin at the peak, and when prices reversed the margin calls forced selling, which forced more margin calls. Between August 1929 and March 1933, the U.S. money supply contracted by more than a third — what Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz would later name the Great Contraction. Wages fell sharply. Debts did not, because they were fixed by contract. Households and farms with cash bought from households and farms without it, at pennies on the dollar. The Depression did not redistribute wealth at random; it transferred it directionally.
anchors.y1933: Banking holiday, deposit insurance, Glass-Steagall, the SEC, public works at scale. The New Deal is the one historical moment in modern U.S. policy when the response to a financial collapse routes value toward the people who lost it rather than the institutions that produced it. Under the postwar policy stack — high top marginal tax rates, strong unions, regulated banking, Bretton Woods — U.S. inequality fell to its lowest measured level. Median household income roughly doubled between 1947 and 1973. In 2008, also on this page, the same kind of crisis would route value the other way. The contrast is the point.
anchors.y1944: Forty-four allied nations meet at a New Hampshire resort and design the postwar monetary order around a U.S. dollar pegged to gold. The IMF and the World Bank are created here. The arrangement holds until 1971, when Nixon ends the gold convertibility — the entry further down this page.
anchors.y1947: The National Security Act creates the CIA. Fifteen years later, the Joint Chiefs would formally propose remote-controlling civilian aircraft and “orchestrating terrorism in U.S. cities” as a pretext for invading Cuba. Kennedy rejected it. The declassified document is filed under this entry.
anchors.y1948-genocide-convention: The Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide is adopted at the United Nations. It protects national, ethnical, racial, and religious groups — and, on Soviet insistence with assent from other states, deliberately does not protect political groups. The exclusion is the legal hinge for almost everything in the rest of Era II: mass killings carried out for political reasons fall outside the convention's reach.
anchors.y1953: Operation Ajax. The CIA and MI6 coordinate the overthrow of Iran's democratically elected prime minister, Mohammad Mosaddegh, after his government nationalizes the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company. The CIA's own internal history, released in 2013, confirms its role as a matter of record.
anchors.y1954: Operation PBSUCCESS. The CIA backs the coup against Jacobo Árbenz of Guatemala — democratically elected, in office two years — under heavy lobbying from the United Fruit Company. The thirty-six-year civil war that follows kills more than 200,000 people. The U.N.-backed truth commission concludes that 83% of the dead were Mayan and 93% of human-rights violations were carried out by state forces. The record below is the citation chain for both halves of that statement.
anchors.y1965: October 1965 through March 1966. The Indonesian army and army-sponsored militias kill an estimated 500,000 to 1,000,000 people associated with the Indonesian Communist Party. The U.S. embassy in Jakarta passes lists of names to the Indonesian generals, knowing they will be used as kill lists. A 1968 CIA research study ranks the result among the worst mass murders of the twentieth century, alongside the Soviet purges and the Nazi exterminations. The CIA's own assessment, declassified, is on this page.
anchors.y1965-vietnam: U.S. ground combat in Vietnam begins in March 1965. By the time the war ends in 1975, the Vietnamese government's own estimate — published in 1995 — places civilian deaths at roughly two million and PAVN/Viet Cong dead at 1.1 million. The war also spills into Cambodia and Laos, neither of them U.S. enemies on paper; the Cambodian death toll runs to 275,000–310,000 and the Laotian to 20,000–62,000. Distributed across three countries, the toll exceeds the population of Berlin.
anchors.y1973: September 11, 1973. The democratically elected Allende government is overthrown in a coup whose covert U.S. financial and operational support was later documented by the Senate Church Committee. The first 9/11 the United States is involved in is the 1973 one.
anchors.y1975: The Church Committee — a U.S. Senate select committee chaired by Frank Church — spends a year examining the conduct of the CIA, FBI, NSA, and IRS. On the hearing-room table: the CIA's silent dart pistol, modified to fire a frozen shellfish-toxin pellet, alongside testimony on Chile, on Cuba, on the Phoenix Program. The Committee's reports remain the single largest declassification of intelligence-agency conduct in U.S. history.
anchors.y1975-condor: Operation Condor. Six South American military regimes — Argentina, Chile, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia, Brazil — run a coordinated, cross-border program of assassination, disappearance, and torture against political opponents, on infrastructure and communications the United States helped supply. Estimates of those killed range from 60,000 to 80,000, with about 400,000 political prisoners taken. The U.S. cables that document the program were declassified in the 1990s and 2000s.
anchors.y1971: Nixon closes the gold window. The Bretton Woods arrangement ends; the dollar becomes a fiat currency backed by nothing but the U.S. government's promise to redeem it. The macroeconomic regime that follows — and the financialization of everything inside it — is the bridge from Era II into the world we live in.
anchors.y1980: Reagan's election ratifies a new economic compact: top marginal tax rates collapse, organized labor is rolled back, financial regulation is loosened, and the social-services baseline begins a thirty-year decline. From 1979 onward, U.S. productivity and the pay of the typical worker — which had moved together since the war — separate; productivity goes on growing while typical pay flattens, and the gap widens. The wealth share of the top 1% rises from roughly 22% in the late 1970s to roughly 34% by 2007. Households make up the difference by borrowing — from the same institutions capturing their productivity gains. The administration's Central America policy, visible on the next entry, runs the same operating system abroad.
anchors.y1980-el-salvador: Twelve years of civil war in El Salvador, 1980–1992, kill approximately 75,000 civilians. The U.N. Truth Commission's report finds that 85% of acts of violence against civilians are carried out by state forces, paramilitaries, and death squads — the same forces the U.S. trains, equips, and funds. The Atlacatl Battalion, U.S.-trained, carries out the 1981 El Mozote massacre. The U.N. report and the declassified U.S. records are filed under this entry.
anchors.y1998-iraq-sanctions: Denis Halliday, the United Nations' Humanitarian Coordinator for Iraq, resigns after thirty-four years at the U.N. rather than continue administering the Security Council's economic sanctions on Iraq. He publicly calls them genocide. His successor, Hans von Sponeck, resigns for the same reason two years later. Halliday cites contemporary estimates of one to one and a half million Iraqi dead from malnutrition and inadequate health care under the sanctions regime. No film has been made about it.
anchors.y2001-attack: An event still being argued about. What follows are the verifiable threads from on and around the day itself — questions about timing, physical evidence, and who knew what when. Silverstein's lease, signed weeks before. Zim's lease, ended weeks before. The Dancing Israelis. The gold in the WTC 4 vault. The records that burned in WTC 7. The passport in the rubble. Hanjour's piloting reports. The Odigo instant messages. The Jennings lobby account. None of these prove anything on their own. They are the questions, with citations.
anchors.y2001-aftermath: What followed, slowly. A CIA officer goes on television in 2007 to confirm the agency tortured. He is prosecuted in 2013. The Senate finally publishes its own study in 2014 — 6,700 pages, of which 525 are made public.
anchors.y2003: The invasion of Iraq, justified by claims about weapons of mass destruction that did not exist. The intelligence apparatus that produced the case is the same one whose conduct the Senate would document in 2014. The casualty figures from this war alone — direct and indirect — clear hundreds of thousands.
anchors.y2008: The global financial crisis. Banks that bought one another's mortgage-backed securities discover they are all insolvent in the same week. Public balance sheets absorb the loss; the executives who built the position keep their bonuses. A 2011 Government Accountability Office audit — the first audit of its kind, mandated by the Dodd–Frank Act — would later document approximately $16 trillion in cumulative gross Federal Reserve emergency lending across the crisis facilities. Asset prices recovered fast; from 2009 to 2012, the top 1% captured 95% of all real-income growth in the recovery (Saez). Roughly eight million U.S. households lost their homes. The bailout's direction is the inverse of the New Deal's. That is the through-line that connects 1933 and 2008 on this page.
anchors.y2010: Citizens United v. FEC. The Supreme Court rules that political spending is speech and that corporations have the same speech rights as people. The post-2010 campaign-finance regime, more than any other single rule change in this era, is what makes the rest of the page legible.
anchors.y2013: Edward Snowden hands the Guardian and the Washington Post a complete archive of NSA bulk-collection programs — PRISM, Boundless Informant, Stellar Wind — and flees the country. The legal arguments the NSA had been making in secret are revealed to have been false in public. The infrastructure he documents is still in operation.
anchors.y2017-bennington: Four high-profile cultural figures — Chris Cornell (Soundgarden, May 2017), Chester Bennington (Linkin Park, July 2017), Avicii (Tim Bergling, April 2018), and Anthony Bourdain (CNN, June 2018) — die in fourteen months. Each death is individually ruled suicide. A claimed documentary tying them together — “The Silent Children” — does not appear in any verifiable production record, but the speculation it represents has circulated continuously since. What is documented is each death itself, and the FBI's own 2007 bulletin on the symbols pedophiles use to identify themselves.
anchors.y2019: Jeffrey Epstein is arrested July 6, 2019. He is found dead in his Manhattan jail cell on August 10. The two guards assigned to his floor sign false log entries. The two cameras pointed at his cell malfunction. By the next year, Ghislaine Maxwell has been arrested and convicted; many of his named clients have not. The opening poll on this page is about what the country thinks happened.
anchors.y2020-pandemic-response: March 2020. The S&P 500 falls 34% in a month. The Federal Reserve expands its balance sheet by approximately $3 trillion in three months — among the fastest such expansions in U.S. monetary history. The market recovers the prior peak within five months. U.S. billionaire wealth increases by approximately $1.3 trillion between March and September 2020. Unemployment peaks at 14.7%, falling hardest on hourly workers with no assets to cushion it. The inflation that follows the stimulus erodes real wages for 25 consecutive months. The rate hikes that end the inflation raise returns for cash-rich savers and raise costs for everyone with a mortgage or small-business loan. Each expansion enriches those nearest the point of credit creation; each contraction transfers assets to those with cash on hand. This is the architecture the 1929 and 2008 entries describe, run faster.
anchors.y2021-surfside: 1:22 a.m., June 24, 2021. The Champlain Towers South in Surfside, Florida partially collapses. 98 dead. NIST traces the cause to the pool deck, the corroded reinforcing steel, and warnings that had been on paper since 2018. Around the same hours, separately and on the other side of the Atlantic, John McAfee dies in a Spanish prison cell. The two threads are real and documented. What ties them together, if anything, is what the rest of the page records.
anchors.y2023: October 7, 2023 begins the latest phase of the conflict in Gaza. The Max Planck Institute for Demographic Research estimates more than 100,000 conflict-related deaths in Gaza by October 2025, with life expectancy falling to nearly half what it would have been without the war. The campaign is funded, armed, and diplomatically shielded by the United States throughout. The Congressional Research Service numbers and U.N. OCHA running totals are filed under this entry.
anchors.y2024-pagers: September 17–18, 2024. Thousands of pagers and walkie-talkies in use by Hezbollah operatives explode at once across Lebanon and Syria. Israel acknowledges responsibility weeks later. A supply-chain assassination at scale — the new precedent invoked any time a different device is later claimed to have done something it didn't.
anchors.y2024: Palantir's defense contracts compound. Its Maven Smart System becomes the U.S. military's targeting backbone. Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, and Amazon Web Services line up on the same side of the same procurement pipeline. The data-center capital expenditure curve — visible on the last entry of this page — is the macroeconomic shape of this build-out.
anchors.y2025-kirk: Charlie Kirk is shot once in the neck at a university debate in Utah, in front of 3,000 people. The accused shooter is identified and charged within 48 hours. The official ballistic, DNA, and witness record is unambiguous. Alternative explanations have circulated — including the claim that an exploding lavalier microphone, not a bullet, was the source of the wound, and assertions that other people in the field of view were involved. Each of those claims is documented under this entry alongside what the official record actually shows.
anchors.y2025: Where the page lands as of mid-2026. The threads above are not isolated; they connect, sometimes loosely and sometimes tightly. The point of presenting them together on one page, with citations, is to make that connection a thing the reader can examine rather than a thing they have to take on faith.
anchors.y2021-anthropic: December 2020. A group of safety researchers — including Dario Amodei (then VP of Research at OpenAI) and his sister Daniela (then VP of Safety & Policy) — leave to start Anthropic. They incorporate as a public-benefit corporation. The stated reason: a different vision for how AI should be scaled, and how the people building it should be trusted.
anchors.y2023-altman-firing: November 17, 2023. The OpenAI board fires Sam Altman, citing a lack of consistent candor in his communications. Five days later he is reinstated. Three of the four board members who voted to remove him are gone within weeks. The board's reasoning — and what was redacted from the public account of it — is the through-line that Ronan Farrow picks up two years later.
anchors.y2026-altman: April 2026. Ronan Farrow and Andrew Marantz publish 16,000 words in the New Yorker, the product of eighteen months of reporting, dozens of named-and-on-record sources, internal memos, and a sitting senior Microsoft executive who compares the man to Madoff and SBF. The article is the receipt.
anchors.y2026-ai-bubble: U.S. AI capital expenditure is running at roughly 5% of GDP. The historical bar at which infrastructure buildouts have busted the economy is 3%. Railroads lasted a century. Highways, fifty years. Fiber, thirty. The GPUs in this round of capex obsolesce every three to four. Daniel Priestley, who called the 2008 crash early, told Steven Bartlett what he's now saying about 2029.
